question: What is Compose Multiplatform and how does it relate to Jetpack Compose?
answer:
Compose Multiplatform (CMP) is JetBrains' port of Jetpack Compose to non-Android targets — iOS, desktop (JVM), and web (Wasm/JS). The composable APIs you know (Column, Row, Modifier, Material 3) work the same way across targets.

Relationship:
• Jetpack Compose — official Android Compose runtime + UI from Google.
• Compose Multiplatform — JetBrains' multiplatform fork, syncs with Compose every release.
• On Android, CMP delegates to Jetpack Compose under the hood.

Maturity:
• Desktop and Android — production-ready since 1.x.
• iOS — stable since 1.6 (2024); recommended for non-trivial use.
• Web (Wasm) — experimental but improving fast.

Key gain: ship a near-identical UI on multiple platforms with one code base. Caveat: native look-and-feel still requires platform-specific tweaks (system fonts, navigation patterns, accessibility hooks).

question: How do you handle resources (images, strings) in Compose Multiplatform?
answer:
CMP ships a Compose-aware resources system in `compose.components.resources`:
• Place files under `commonMain/composeResources/`:
  - `drawable/` for images.
  - `font/` for typefaces.
  - `values/strings.xml` for strings (Android-style format).
  - `values-ru/strings.xml`, `values-de/...` for locales.
• Generated `Res` Kotlin object exposes typed accessors: `Res.drawable.logo`, `Res.string.hello`, `Res.font.sora_bold`.
• Use them in composables via `painterResource(...)`, `stringResource(...)`, `Font(Res.font...)`.

Fonts and images are bundled in the platform binary — no need to copy them separately per target. For very large images consider lazy / on-demand loading from network with Coil 3 (multiplatform).

question: How do you bridge platform-specific UI into Compose Multiplatform?
answer:
Sometimes you need a real native widget (camera preview, MapKit, AVPlayer, WebView). CMP supports interop on each platform:

• Android — `AndroidView { factory }` to host a View / `ComposeView` already inherits Compose, so most things work.
• iOS — `UIKitView { factory }` and `UIKitViewController { factory }` (since CMP 1.6) to host UIKit views / SwiftUI hosted via UIHostingController.
• Desktop — `SwingPanel { factory }` for Swing/AWT components.
• Web — DOM interop is more limited; embed via `Element` factories.

Keep the embedded view as small / specific as possible — the styling, navigation, and back-press handling stays your responsibility.

question: How do you handle navigation in Compose Multiplatform?
answer:
Several options, in order of maturity:

• Compose Multiplatform Navigation (Jetpack Navigation Compose ported) — recommended path going forward; supports `NavHost`, `composable(...)`, type-safe routes, deep links. Available as `androidx.navigation:navigation-compose-multiplatform` (Beta in 2024).
• Decompose — battle-tested KMP navigation library predating CMP. State-machine model with components, routes, and back-stack management. Strong for complex flows; verbose for simple ones.
• Voyager — comparatively lightweight, type-safe screen API; popular in CMP apps.
• Native navigation per platform — wrap CMP screens in NavigationStack (iOS) / NavGraph (Android) and use shared ViewModels for state.

Pick by app complexity. For 5-screen apps Voyager / Compose Nav is enough; for large apps or when deep links matter heavily, Decompose pays off.

question: What does iOS look-and-feel cost? When NOT to use Compose Multiplatform?
answer:
CMP renders pixel-perfect on iOS using Skia, NOT UIKit. The result feels Compose-native, not iOS-native. Things that drift from native iOS:

• System fonts and dynamic type sizing — you must opt into them; Material 3's defaults look Material, not iOS.
• Native NavigationStack chrome (large title, back swipe gesture, sheets) — CMP's navigation has improved in 1.6+ but still feels off in subtle ways.
• Accessibility plumbing differs slightly; VoiceOver hooks need testing.
• System gestures (pull-to-refresh, swipe-to-delete) are Compose implementations, not UIKit.

When NOT to use CMP:
• Apps where 'feels iOS-native' is a strict product requirement (banking, premium consumer brands).
• Apps that integrate deeply with iOS-only features (HealthKit complications, Live Activities).
• Tiny apps with little shared logic.

When CMP shines: design-system-driven apps where the brand DOES want a consistent look across platforms; internal tools, admin apps, content apps with custom UI.

question: How do testing, build size, and tooling compare for CMP?
answer:
Testing:
• Common composables tests via `androidx.compose.ui:ui-test-junit4` (Android) and Compose Multiplatform's `runComposeUiTest` for desktop / iOS (`@OptIn(ExperimentalTestApi::class)`). Same APIs (`onNodeWithText`, etc.).
• Unit tests for ViewModels run on JVM — no platform overhead.

Build size:
• iOS framework with Compose UI is ~10–15 MB compressed (depends on Skia engines, fonts). Larger than a hand-rolled Swift app, smaller than Flutter's engine.
• Android benefits from being already-Compose; minimal extra weight.
• Desktop: 30+ MB JVM bundle is typical (Skiko + JRE).

Tooling:
• Android Studio + Kotlin Multiplatform plugin handles edits and previews on Android. iOS previews still require building via Xcode.
• Hot reload — improving but slower than native SwiftUI's instant preview.
• Debugging: stack traces span Kotlin → ObjC frames; `lldb` works but Swift symbols can be noisy.
• CI: each target needs its own runner (Linux for Android/Desktop, macOS for iOS); plan accordingly.

Trade-off: CMP is a step up vs vanilla KMP — closer to one-codebase ideal — but still costs more in tooling discipline than mature single-platform stacks.
